blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلسَّمَآءِ ذَاتِ ٱلْبُرُوجِ (١)
By the heaven with its Houses, (1)
وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْمَوْعُودِ (٢)
by the Promised Day, (2)
وَشَاهِدٍ وَمَشْهُودٍ (٣)
by the Witness and the Witnessed: (3)
قُتِلَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْأُخْدُودِ (٤)
perish the People of the Ditch! (4)
ٱلنَّارِ ذَاتِ ٱلْوَقُودِ (٥)
The fire abounding in fuel, (5)
إِذْ هُمْ عَلَيْهَا قُعُودٌ (٦)
above which they sat (6)
وَهُمْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَفْعَلُونَ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ شُهُودٌ (٧)
as they were themselves witnesses to what they did to the faithful. (7)
وَمَا نَقَمُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ إِلَّآ أَن يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَمِيدِ (٨)
They were vindictive towards them only because they had faith in Allah, the All-mighty and the All-laudable, (8)
ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ (٩)
to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and Allah is witness to all things. (9)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ فَتَنُوا۟ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَتُوبُوا۟ فَلَهُمْ عَذَابُ جَهَنَّمَ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابُ ٱلْحَرِيقِ (١٠)
Indeed those who persecute the faithful, men and women, and do not repent thereafter, there is the punishment of hell for them and the punishment of burning. (10)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْكَبِيرُ (١١)
Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds—for them will be gardens with streams running in them. That is the supreme success. (11)
إِنَّ بَطْشَ رَبِّكَ لَشَدِيدٌ (١٢)
Indeed your Lord’s striking is severe. (12)
إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ يُبْدِئُ وَيُعِيدُ (١٣)
It is indeed He who originates and brings back, (13)
وَهُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلْوَدُودُ (١٤)
and He is the All-forgiving, the All-affectionate, (14)
ذُو ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْمَجِيدُ (١٥)
Lord of the Throne, the All-glorious, (15)
فَعَّالٌ لِّمَا يُرِيدُ (١٦)
doer of what He desires. (16)
هَلْ أَتَىٰكَ حَدِيثُ ٱلْجُنُودِ (١٧)
Did you receive the story of the hosts (17)
فِرْعَوْنَ وَثَمُودَ (١٨)
of Pharaoh and Thamud? (18)
بَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فِى تَكْذِيبٍ (١٩)
Indeed the faithless dwell in denial, (19)
وَٱللَّهُ مِن وَرَآئِهِم مُّحِيطٌۢ (٢٠)
and Allah besieges them from all around. (20)
بَلْ هُوَ قُرْءَانٌ مَّجِيدٌ (٢١)
Indeed it is a glorious Quran, (21)
فِى لَوْحٍ مَّحْفُوظٍۭ (٢٢)
in a preserved tablet. (22)

(Commentary)

This is a Surah of warning and glad tidings, containing a severe threat to those who persecute (يفتنون) — subjecting to trial and torment — the faithful men and women because of their faith in Allah, just as the polytheists (المشركون) among the people of Mecca used to do to those who believed in the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). They would torture them to force them back to their previous polytheism; some remained patient and did not recant, no matter how dire the situation became, while others recanted and apostatized due to the weakness of their faith. This is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَقُولُ آمَنَّا بِاللهِ فَإِذا أُوذِيَ فِي اللهِ جَعَلَ فِتْنَةَ النَّاسِ كَعَذابِ اللهِ — "Among the people are those who say, ‘We believe in Allah,’ but if they are made to suffer for the sake of Allah, they take the persecution of the people to be like Allah’s punishment" (29:10), and His saying: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَعْبُدُ اللهَ عَلى حَرْفٍ فَإِنْ أَصابَهُ خَيْرٌ اطْمَأَنَّ بِهِ وَإِنْ أَصابَتْهُ فِتْنَةٌ انْقَلَبَ عَلى وَجْهِهِ — "And among the people are those who worship Allah on the verge: if good fortune befalls him, he is content with it; but if an ordeal (فتنة) befalls him, he turns about on his face" (22:11).

Before addressing this, the Glorified preceded it with an allusion to the story of the People of the Ditch (أصحاب الأخدود), which serves to incite the believers toward patience for the sake of Allah, the Exalted. He followed it with an allusion to the account of the hosts (الجنود) — Pharaoh and Thamud — which serves to comfort the soul of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with the promise of victory and to threaten the polytheists.

The Surah is Meccan (مكية), as evidenced by the context (سياق) of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالسَّماءِ ذاتِ الْبُرُوجِ — "By the heaven with its constellations (البروج)" (85:1). Buruj (البروج) is the plural of burj (برج), which refers to something manifest (الأمر الظاهر). It is most commonly used for a high palace (القصر العالي) due to its prominence to onlookers. The structures built upon a city wall for defense are also called towers (برجا), and this is what is intended in the verse, based on His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ جَعَلْنا فِي السَّماءِ بُرُوجاً وَزَيَّنَّاها لِلنَّاظِرِينَ وَحَفِظْناها مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْطانٍ رَجِيمٍ — "Certainly We have appointed constellations in the sky and adorned them for the beholders, and We have guarded them from every outcast Satan" (15:16-17). Thus, the "constellations" (البروج) refer to the positions of the stars in the heaven.

From this, it becomes clear that interpreting the buruj as the twelve signs of the zodiac (البروج الاثني عشر) as defined in the science of astronomy (علم النجوم) is not sound. The verse contains an oath by the heaven that is protected by these constellations. The suitability of this to the story that will be alluded to, as well as the subsequent threat and promise, is not hidden, and we shall point it out.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْيَوْمِ الْمَوْعُودِ — "and by the promised day" (85:2). This is conjoined to "the heaven" and is an oath by the Promised Day, which is the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة) wherein Allah has promised to judge between His servants.

His saying, the Exalted: وَشاهِدٍ وَمَشْهُودٍ — "and by the witness (شاهد) and the witnessed (مشهود)" (85:3). These are conjoined to "the heaven," and the whole sequence constitutes oath after oath regarding what is intended to be clarified in the Surah—which, as previously mentioned, is the severe threat to those who persecute the faithful men and women for their faith, and the beautiful promise to those who believe and perform righteous deeds (الصالحات).

It is as if it were said: I swear by the heaven with its constellations through which Allah repels the devils, that Allah shall repel from the faith of the believers the plot of the devils and their allies among the disbelievers; and I swear by the Promised Day in which people will be recompensed for their deeds; and I swear by a witness (شاهد) who witnesses and beholds the deeds of those disbelievers and what they do to the believers because of their faith in Allah; and I swear by a witnessed (مشهود) event that everyone shall witness and behold, that indeed those who persecuted the faithful men and women... to the end of the two verses.

From here, it appears that "witnessing" (الشهادة) in "witness" (شاهد) and "witnessed" (مشهود) carries a single meaning, which is beholding through presence (المعاينة بالحضور). Furthermore, if it were in the sense of "bearing witness" (تأدية الشهادة), the appropriate expression would have been "witnessed against" (مشهود عليه), because in that sense, the verb is transitive with the preposition 'ala (على).

Accordingly, "witness" (شاهد) can be applied to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) due to his witnessing the deeds of his community (أمة) and then testifying over them on the Day of Resurrection. "Witnessed" (مشهود) can be applied to the torture the disbelievers inflicted upon these believers and the persecution they subjected them to; or if you wish, to their recompense; or if you wish, to the punishment and reward that will occur on the Day of Resurrection for these oppressors and the oppressed. The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "witnessed" and "witness" is, in any case, for magnification (التفخيم).

Exegesis regarding "witness" and "witnessed" contains many opinions, which some have numbered up to thirty. Examples include: the witness is Friday and the witnessed is the Day of Arafah; the witness is the Day of Sacrifice and the witnessed is the Day of Arafah; the witness is the Day of Arafah and the witnessed is the Day of Resurrection; the witness is the angel who witnesses against the children of Adam and the witnessed is the Day of Resurrection; the witness is those who testify against the people and the witnessed is those testified against.

Other opinions include: the witness is this community and the witnessed is the rest of the nations; the witness is the limbs of the children of Adam and the witnessed is their souls; the witness is the Black Stone and the witnessed is the pilgrim; the witness is the days and nights and the witnessed is the children of Adam; the witness is the prophets and the witnessed is Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); the witness is Allah and the witnessed is "There is no god but Allah"; the witness is the creation and the witnessed is the Truth (الحق); the witness is Allah and the witnessed is the Day of Resurrection; the witness is Adam and his progeny and the witnessed is the Day of Resurrection; the witness is the Day of Tarwiyah and the witnessed is the Day of Arafah; they are Monday and Friday; the witness is those brought near (المقربون) and the witnessed is Illiyyun (عليون); the witness is the infant who said to his mother in the story of the Ditch, "Be patient, for you are upon the truth," and the witnessed is the event itself; the witness is the successive angels who record deeds and the witnessed is the dawn prayer (قرآن الفجر); among other such statements.

Most of these opinions, as you can see, are based on taking "witnessing" (الشهادة) in the sense of "delivering a testimony" (أداء ما حمل من الشهادة), and some are based on differentiating between the witness and the witnessed in the meaning of witnessing, the weakness of which has already been noted. What is most suitable for the context is to take it in the sense of beholding (المعاينة), even if that necessitates witnessing in the sense of delivering testimony on the Day of Resurrection, and that "witness" (شاهد) is applicable to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

How could it not be so? Allah, the Exalted, has named him a witness (شاهدا) when He said: يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ إِنَّا أَرْسَلْناكَ شاهِداً وَمُبَشِّراً وَنَذِيراً — "O Prophet! Indeed We have sent you as a witness, as a bearer of good news and as a warner" (33:45), and He named him a witness (شهيدا) when He said: لِيَكُونَ الرَّسُولُ شَهِيداً عَلَيْكُمْ — "so that the Apostle may be a witness to you" (22:78). We have already explained the meaning of the "witnessing of deeds" (شهادة الأعمال) by their witnesses in previous sections.

Furthermore, the response to the oath (جواب القسم) is omitted (محذوف), indicated by His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ فَتَنُوا الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ — "Indeed those who persecute the faithful men and women..." (85:10) until the end of the two verses. It is also alluded to by His saying: قُتِلَ أَصْحابُ الْأُخْدُودِ — "Perish the People of the Ditch!" (85:4) etc. It is a threat to the persecutors and a promise to the righteous believers, and an assurance that Allah will grant them success in patience and support them in preserving their faith against the plots of the plotters if they are sincere, just as He did with the believers in the story of the Ditch.

His saying, the Exalted: قُتِلَ أَصْحابُ الْأُخْدُودِ — "Perish the People of the Ditch!" (85:4). This is an allusion to the story of the Ditch to serve as a preamble and preparation for what follows in His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ فَتَنُوا — "Indeed those who persecute..." etc. It is by no means the response to the oath.

The ukhdud (الأخدود) is a great trench or cleft in the earth. The "People of the Ditch" are the tyrants (الجبابرة) who dug a ditch, kindled a fire within it, and ordered the believers to enter it, burning them all as a vengeance (نقما) for their faith. Thus, His saying قُتِلَ — "Perish" — is an invocation against them, meaning they are cursed (اللعن) and expelled from mercy.

It has been said that the "People of the Ditch" refers to the faithful men and women who were burned in it, and His saying قُتِلَ is a report (إخبار) of their being killed by burning, not an invocation. This is weakened by the apparent reference of the pronouns in His saying: إِذْ هُمْ عَلَيْها — "as they sat by it" (85:6), هُمْ عَلى ما يَفْعَلُونَ — "they were witnesses to what they were doing" (85:7), and ما نَقَمُوا — "They were vindictive toward them only because..." (85:8) to the People of the Ditch. These pronouns, especially in the second and third instances, refer to the vindictive tyrants, not the tormented believers.

His saying, the Exalted: النَّارِ ذاتِ الْوَقُودِ — "the fire abounding in fuel" (85:5). This is an appositive (بدل) for "the Ditch." Waqud (الوقود) is that with which fire is kindled, such as wood and the like. Describing the fire as "abounding in fuel" (ذات الوقود) points to the magnitude of this fire and the intensity of its blaze and roar.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ هُمْ عَلَيْها قُعُودٌ — "as they sat by it" (85:6). That is, while those tyrants were sitting at the edges of the fire, overlooking it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُمْ عَلى ما يَفْعَلُونَ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ شُهُودٌ — "and they were witnesses to what they were doing to the faithful" (85:7). That is, they were present, watching and witnessing their burning and incineration.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما نَقَمُوا مِنْهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ يُؤْمِنُوا بِاللهِ — "They were vindictive toward them only because they had faith in Allah" (85:8). Naqam (النقم) with two fathahs means intense dislike (الكراهة الشديدة). That is, they disliked nothing about those believers except their faith in Allah, so they burned them because of their faith.

His saying, the Exalted: الْعَزِيزِ الْحَمِيدِ الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَاللهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ — "the All-mighty, the All-laudable, to whom belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and Allah is witness to all things" (85:8-9). These are attributes following the Name of Majesty (اسم الجلالة), pointing to the proof that those believers were upon the truth in their faith and were oppressed in what was done to them; their state is not hidden from Allah, and He will reward them with the best reward. It also proves that those tyrants were upon falsehood, audacious against Allah, and unjust in what they did, and they shall taste the evil consequence of their affair. This is because He, the Exalted, is Allah, the All-mighty (العزيز) — the Absolute Victor who is never overcome — and the All-laudable (الحميد) — the one whose acts are absolutely beautiful. To Him alone belongs all majesty (الجلال) and beauty (الجمال), so it is mandatory to submit to Him and not to violate His sanctity. Since to Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, He is the Absolute Sovereign (المليك) to whom belongs the command and the judgment; He is the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين). Thus, it is mandatory to take Him as the God to be worshipped (إلها معبودا) and not to associate anyone with Him. Therefore, the believers in Him are upon the truth, and the disbelievers are in straying (ضلال).

Furthermore, Allah—who is the Originator (الموجد) of everything—is a witness (شهيد) over everything. Nothing of His creation or their deeds is hidden from Him. Neither the goodness of the doer of good nor the evil of the evildoer is veiled from Him, and He shall recompense each according to what they have done.

In short, since He, the Exalted, is Allah, characterized by these noble attributes, it was incumbent upon these believers to believe in Him, and it was not for those tyrants to interfere with their state or touch them with harm.

Some exegetes said regarding the purpose of mentioning these attributes in the verse: If the people were polytheists, then what they were vindictive toward and denied regarding the believers was not faith in Allah, the Exalted, but rather the denial of their false deities. If they were atheists (معطلة), then what they denied was nothing but the affirmation of a deity unknown to them. However, since the outcome of both matters is the denial of the True Deity (المعبود الحق) described with attributes of majesty and honor, He expressed it as He did by applying these attributes to Himself, the Exalted.

In this, there is an oversight of the fact that the polytheists—namely the idolaters (الوثنية)—did not attribute to Allah, the Exalted, anything except creation and origination (الصنع والإيجاد). As for Lordship (الربوبية) which entails governance (التدبير), and Divinity (الألوهية) which necessitates worship (العبادة), they restricted these to their lords and gods, worshipping them instead of Allah, the Glorified. To them, He was nothing more than the Lord of lords and the God of gods.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ فَتَنُوا الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَتُوبُوا فَلَهُمْ عَذابُ جَهَنَّمَ وَلَهُمْ عَذابُ الْحَرِيقِ — "Indeed those who persecute the faithful men and women, and then do not repent, for them is the punishment of hell, and for them is the punishment of burning" (85:10). Fitnah (الفتنة) is trial and torture. "Those who persecute" (الَّذِينَ فَتَنُوا) etc., is general, encompassing the People of the Ditch and the polytheists of the Quraysh who used to persecute the faithful men and women who believed in the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with various types of torment to make them recant their religion.

It is said in al-Majma': A question is asked: How is the punishment of Hell distinguished from the punishment of burning when they are one? The answer is that it means they have various types of punishment in Hell besides burning, such as Zaqqum (الزقوم), Ghislin (الغسلين), and maces (المقامع), and they have, in addition to that, the burning by fire. End quote.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَهُمْ جَنَّاتٌ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ ذلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْكَبِيرُ — "Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds, for them are gardens with conduits running amidst them. That is the supreme success" (85:11). This is a beautiful promise to the believers to comfort their souls, just as what preceded it was a severe threat to the persecuting, tormenting disbelievers.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ بَطْشَ رَبِّكَ لَشَدِيدٌ — "Indeed your Lord’s striking is severe" (85:12). This verse, until the end of seven verses, is a verification and emphasis of the preceding threat and promise. Batsh (البطش) — as mentioned by al-Raghib — is to seize something with might (صولة).

In the attribution of "striking" (البطش) to "the Lord" (الرب) and the attribution of "the Lord" to the second-person singular (the Prophet), there is a comforting of the soul of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with support and victory, and an indication that the tyrants of his community have a share in the aforementioned threat.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُ هُوَ يُبْدِئُ وَيُعِيدُ — "It is He who certainly begins and brings back" (85:13). The contrast between "the Beginner" (المبدئ) and "the Bringer-back" (المعيد) implies that ibda' (الإبداء) means beginning and initiating a thing. They said: ibda' has not been heard from the Arabs, but the recitation is thus; in some anomalous recitations (القراءات الشاذة), it is yabda'u (يبدأ) with a fathah on the ya and dal.

In any case, the verse is a justification for the severity of His striking. This is because He, the Exalted, is the Beginner (مبدئ) who brings into existence whatever He wills of a thing as an initial creation without seeking aid from anything other than Himself. And He, the Exalted, brings back (يعيد) everything that was to what it was, and every state that passed to what it was before passing. Thus, nothing He wills is impossible for Him, and nothing that vanishes escapes Him. Since this is so, He is capable of inflicting upon the servant who transgresses his limit a punishment that is beyond his limit and beyond his capacity, while preserving him as he is so that he may taste the punishment. He, the Exalted, said: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَهُمْ نارُ جَهَنَّمَ لا يُقْضى عَلَيْهِمْ فَيَمُوتُوا وَلا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمْ مِنْ عَذابِها — "As for the faithless, for them is the fire of hell: they will neither be finished off so that they may die, nor will its punishment be lightened for them" (35:36).

He is also capable of restoring what the punishment has destroyed to its original state so that the criminal may taste the punishment without interruption. He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِنا سَوْفَ نُصْلِيهِمْ ناراً كُلَّما نَضِجَتْ جُلُودُهُمْ بَدَّلْناهُمْ جُلُوداً غَيْرَها لِيَذُوقُوا الْعَذابَ — "Indeed those who deny Our signs, We shall soon make them enter a Fire: as often as their skins are scorched, We shall replace them with other skins, so that they may taste the punishment" (4:56).

Through this explanation, it becomes clear:

First: That the context of His saying إِنَّهُ هُوَ — "It is He who certainly..." etc., implies restriction (القصر), meaning that the initiation of existence and its restoration belong to Allah, the Glorified, alone, since creation and origination terminate in Him alone.

Second: That the limits of things belong to Him, the Exalted; if He willed not to limit them, they would not be limited, and if He willed to change one limit for another, He would. He is the one who limited punishment and trial in this world by death and cessation, and had He not willed it, He would not have limited it, as is the case with the punishment of the Hereafter.

Third: That the meaning of the "severity of striking" (شدة البطش) — which is seizing with violence — is that there is no repeller of His seizing and no rejecter of His judgment, however He judges, unless another judgment from Him intervenes between His judgment and its object, thereby restricting the first.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الْغَفُورُ الْوَدُودُ — "and He is the All-forgiving, the All-affectionate" (85:14). That is, the one of much forgiveness and affection. This looks toward the promise to the believers, just as His saying إِنَّ بَطْشَ رَبِّكَ — "Indeed your Lord’s striking..." etc., looks toward the threat to the disbelievers.

His saying, the Exalted: ذُو الْعَرْشِ الْمَجِيدُ فَعَّالٌ لِما يُرِيدُ — "Lord of the Throne, the All-glorious, Doer of whatever He wills" (85:15-16). The Throne (العرش) is the throne of sovereignty, and "Lord of the Throne" is a metonymy for the King (الملك); that is, He is the King who has the right to dispose of His kingdom however He disposes and to judge as He wills. "The All-glorious" (المجيد) is an attribute from majd (المجد), which is spiritual greatness (العظمة المعنوية), namely the perfection of essence and attributes. His saying فَعَّالٌ لِما يُرِيدُ — "Doer of whatever He wills" — means that no diverter turns Him away from what He wills, neither from within due to annoyance, laziness, boredom, or change of will, nor from without due to an obstacle that comes between Him and what He wills.

Thus, He, the Exalted, has the right to threaten those who persecuted the faithful men and women with the Fire, and to promise those who believe and do righteous deeds with Paradise, for He is the Lord of the Throne, the All-glorious, and He shall never break His promise, for He is the Doer of whatever He wills.

His saying, the Exalted: هَلْ أَتاكَ حَدِيثُ الْجُنُودِ فِرْعَوْنَ وَثَمُودَ — "Did you receive the story of the hosts, of Pharaoh and Thamud?" (85:17-18). This is an affirmation of what preceded regarding the severity of His striking and His being the All-glorious King, the Doer of whatever He wills. In it is consolation for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and a comforting of his noble soul by alluding to their account. The meaning of the two verses is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي تَكْذِيبٍ — "Rather the faithless are in [a state of] denial" (85:19). It is not unlikely to infer from the context that "those who disbelieve" refers to the people of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

The verse contains a transition (إضراب) from the preceding exhortation and proof in terms of their effect. The meaning is: faith should not be expected from them through these clear signs, for those who disbelieve are persistent in their denial and do not benefit from exhortation or proof.

From here, it becomes clear that the meaning of "those who disbelieve are in denial" (فِي تَكْذِيبٍ) — using the container-metaphor of denial — is their persistence in it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ مِنْ وَرائِهِمْ مُحِيطٌ — "while Allah encompasses them from all sides" (85:20). "Behind" (وراء) a thing refers to the external directions surrounding it. This is an indication that they cannot frustrate Allah, the Glorified, for He encompasses them and is capable over them from every side. In it is also a comforting of the soul of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

It is narrated from some that in His saying مِنْ وَرائِهِمْ — "from behind them" — there is a subtle hint that they have cast Allah behind their backs (ظهريا), which is based on taking wara' (وراء) in the sense of "behind."

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ هُوَ قُرْآنٌ مَجِيدٌ فِي لَوْحٍ مَحْفُوظٍ — "Indeed it is a glorious Quran, in a guarded tablet (لوح محفوظ)" (85:21-22). This is a transition from their persistence in denying the Quran. The meaning is: the matter is not as they claim; rather, the Quran is a recited book, great in its meaning, abundant in its knowledge, in a tablet guarded (لوح محفوظ) against falsehood and vanity, protected from the touch of the devils.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Jabir ibn Abdillah that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was asked about السَّماءِ ذاتِ الْبُرُوجِ and he said: "The stars." He was asked about الَّذِي جَعَلَ فِي السَّماءِ بُرُوجاً and he said: "The stars." It was said: "Then what about بُرُوجٍ مُشَيَّدَةٍ (lofty towers)?" He said: "Palaces."

In the same source, Abd ibn Humayd, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Abi al-Dunya in al-Usul, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan narrated from Abu Hurayrah that he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "The Promised Day is the Day of Resurrection; the Witnessed Day is the Day of Arafah; and the Witness is Friday." (The hadith).

I say: It has been narrated similarly through other chains from Abu Malik, Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib, and Jubayr ibn Mut'im from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The wording of the latter is: "The Witness is Friday and the Witnessed is the Day of Arafah."

This wording was also narrated from Abd al-Razzaq, al-Faryabi, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn al-Mundhir from Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام).

In the same source, Abd ibn Humayd and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ali (عليه السلام) that he said: "The Promised Day is the Day of Resurrection, the Witness is Friday, and the Witnessed is the Day of Sacrifice."

In al-Majma', it is narrated: A man entered the Mosque of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and found a man narrating from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). He said: I asked him about the Witness and the Witnessed, and he said: "Yes, the Witness is Friday and the Witnessed is the Day of Arafah." I passed him to another narrating from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and asked him about that, and he said: "As for the Witness, it is Friday, and as for the Witnessed, it is the Day of Sacrifice."

I passed them both to a youth whose face was like a gold coin (الدينار), and he was narrating from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). I said: "Inform me about the Witness and the Witnessed." He said: "Yes, as for the Witness, it is Muhammad, and as for the Witnessed, it is the Day of Resurrection. Have you not heard Allah, the Glorified, saying: يا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ إِنَّا أَرْسَلْناكَ شاهِداً وَمُبَشِّراً وَنَذِيراً (O Prophet! Indeed We have sent you as a witness, as a bearer of good news and as a warner), and He said: ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَجْمُوعٌ لَهُ النَّاسُ وَذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَشْهُودٌ (That is a day on which mankind will be gathered, and that is a day witnessed)?"

I asked about the first, and they said: "Ibn Abbas." I asked about the second, and they said: "Ibn Amr." I asked about the third, and they said: "al-Hasan ibn Ali."

I say: The hadith is narrated through various chains and with similar wordings. It has already been mentioned in the exegesis of the verse that what he (peace be upon him) mentioned is more apparent considering the context of the verses, even if the terms "witness" and "witnessed" do not preclude application to others as well in some way.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: قُتِلَ أَصْحابُ الْأُخْدُودِ, he said: Its cause was that the one who incited the Abyssinians to invade Yemen was Dhu Nuwas—the last of the kings of Himyar. He converted to Judaism, and Himyar joined him in Judaism. He named himself Yusuf and remained thus for a period of time.

Then he was informed that in Najran there were remnants of a people upon the religion of Christianity—they were upon the religion of Jesus and the judgment of the Gospel, and the head of that religion was Abdillah ibn Biryamin. His coreligionists urged him to march against them and force them into Judaism. He marched until he reached Najran, gathered those who were upon the religion of Christianity, and offered them Judaism and entry into it. They refused him, so he argued with them and offered it again, being extremely persistent, but they refused and abstained from entering Judaism, choosing death instead.

So he dug a ditch for them, gathered wood in it, and kindled fire. Some of them were burned by fire, and some were killed by the sword, and he mutilated them with every kind of mutilation. The number of those killed and burned by fire reached twenty thousand. One man escaped them, called Dawsh Dhu Tha'laban, on a swift horse of his. They pursued him until he outpaced them in the sands. Dhu Nuwas returned to his deeds among his troops, and Allah said: قُتِلَ أَصْحابُ الْأُخْدُودِ — until His saying — الْعَزِيزِ الْحَمِيدِ.

In al-Majma', it is narrated that Sa'id ibn Jubayr said: When the people of Isfandahan were defeated, Umar ibn al-Khattab said: "They are neither Jews nor Christians, nor do they have a book; they were Magians." Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام) said: "On the contrary, they did have a book that was taken away."

That was because a king of theirs became intoxicated and fell upon his daughter—or he said: upon his sister. When he sobered up, he said to her: "How is the way out of what I have fallen into?" She said: "Gather the people of your kingdom and inform them that you deem the marriage of daughters permissible, and order them to permit it." He gathered them and informed them, but they refused to follow him. So he dug a ditch for them in the earth, kindled fires in it, and presented them to it. Whoever refused to accept that, he cast into the fire, and whoever agreed, he let go.

I say: This meaning is narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Abd ibn Humayd from him (peace be upon him).

From Tafsir al-Ayyashi, with its chain from Jabir from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), he said: Ali (peace be upon him) sent for the Bishop of Najran to ask him about the People of the Ditch. He informed him of something, and he (peace be upon him) said: "It is not as you mentioned, but I will inform you about them:

Indeed, Allah sent an Abyssinian man as a prophet, and they were Abyssinians. They denied him, so he fought them, and they killed his companions and captured him and his companions. Then they built a wall (حيرا) for him, filled it with fire, and gathered the people. They said: 'Whoever is upon our religion and our command, let him step aside; and whoever is upon the religion of these, let him throw himself into the fire.' His companions began throwing themselves into the fire. Then a woman came with a month-old infant of hers. When she approached, she hesitated and felt pity for her son. The infant called out: 'Do not hesitate! Throw me and yourself into the fire, for this, by Allah, is little for the sake of Allah.' So she threw herself and her infant into the fire. He was among those who spoke in the cradle."

I say: This meaning is narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Mardawayh from Abdillah ibn Naji from him (peace be upon him). It is also narrated from Ibn Abi Hatim by way of Abdillah ibn Naji from him (peace be upon him) that he said: "The prophet of the People of the Ditch was an Abyssinian."

It is also narrated from Ibn Abi Hatim and Ibn al-Mundhir by way of al-Hasan from him (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَصْحابُ الْأُخْدُودِ, he said: "They are the Abyssinians."

It is not unlikely to infer that the account of the People of the Ditch refers to multiple events that occurred in Abyssinia, Yemen, and Persia, and the allusion in the verse is to all of them. There are narrations that tell the story while remaining silent about the location of its occurrence.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ هُوَ قُرْآنٌ مَجِيدٌ فِي لَوْحٍ مَحْفُوظٍ, he said: "The Guarded Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) has two ends: one end is on the right of the Throne, on the forehead of Israfil. When the Lord, majestic is His mention, speaks with revelation (الوحي), the Tablet strikes the forehead of Israfil, so he looks into the Tablet and reveals what is in the Tablet to Gabriel."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu al-Shaykh and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas that he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Allah created a Tablet from a white pearl, its two covers are of green emerald; its writing is of light. He glances at it every day three hundred and sixty times; He gives life and brings death, He creates and provides, He honors and humbles, and He does whatever He wills."

I say: The narrations regarding the description of the Tablet are many and varied, and they are of the nature of symbolic representation (تمثيل).
